[[specification]]
== Specification

After generating an in-toto attestation, 

PICTURE HERE OF WHAT CHOICES PEOPLE HAVE.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Depending on how the in-toto attestation is created (i.e., the specific tooling), you

=== Option 1: Store the attestation besides the artifact that is being attested

HIGH-LEVEL GRAPH OF WHAT IT MEANS TO STORE AN ATTESTATION BESIDES AN ARTIFACT.

==== Case study: Repository Release

===== Producer


===== Consumer


==== Case study: npm

===== Producer/Signer

Many ways exist to produce and store attestations within npm.
For example, if within a GitHub Workflow, the development team gives a GitHub Workflow a write token, run the workflow on a GitHub hosted runner, and publiblish the package with the ``--provenance`` field. 
Such a workflow would look as follows:

```
name: Publish Package to npmjs
on:
  release:
    types: [published]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      id-token: write
    steps:
...
      - run: npm publish --provenance
```

The package when published then creates a [SLSA build provenance](https://slsa.dev/spec/v1.2/build-provenance) attestation that describes how the software was built. 
In this case, what the attestation would attest to would be how the npm package was built through a GitHub Workflow (the one previously defined) in a specific repository.
Each attestation that is created would be signed through [Sigstore Rekor](https://docs.sigstore.dev/logging/overview/) signed by the OIDC issuer (https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com) creating an X.509 certificate.
The certificate then contains the data specific within the [Sigstore OID](https://github.com/sigstore/fulcio/blob/main/docs/oid-info.md) documentation.
Each attestation with the certificate is stored as a [Sigstore Bundle](https://docs.sigstore.dev/about/bundle/) besides the artifact that is being attested to.

===== Consumer/Verifier

To find an attestation for a package, you can request the artifacts for the specific version. 
For example, to get the attestations for `pkg:npm/axios@1.15.2` you can query the following endpoint
`https://registry.npmjs.org/-/npm/v1/attestations/axios@1.15.2`.

We then get the following two attestations (`https://github.com/npm/attestation/tree/main/specs/publish/v0.1`) and (`https://slsa.dev/provenance/v1`) 
that are stored in [Sigstore Bundles](https://docs.sigstore.dev/about/bundle/).

```JSON
{
  "attestations": [
    ...
    {
      "predicateType": "https://slsa.dev/provenance/v1",
      "bundle": {
        "mediaType": "application/vnd.dev.sigstore.bundle.v0.3+json",
        "verificationMaterial": {
          "certificate": {
            "rawBytes": ""
          },
          "tlogEntries": [
            ...
          ],
          "timestampVerificationData": {
            "rfc3161Timestamps": []
          }
        },
        "dsseEnvelope": {
          "payload": "",
          "payloadType": "application/vnd.in-toto+json",
          "signatures": [
            {
              "sig": "MEQCIC1ywJw4KeZ+9Jkru1JE9CZm7JbZjGLaufYME755XKA3AiAXOY2kiAcU1WPid0FGVFz99emttlukmd7Cb/780cquDw==",
              "keyid": ""
            }
          ]
        }
      },
      "signedAccessSignatureUrl": ""
    }
  ]
}
```


=== Option 2: Storage in a database

==== Case study: GitHub Attestations


Attestations generated using the GitHub Workflows: [`attest`](https://github.com/actions/attest), [`attest-build-provenance`](https://github.com/actions/attest-build-provenance), and [`attest-sbom`](https://github.com/actions/attest-sbom) are stored in `https://github.com/{ORG}/{REPOSITORY}/attestations` for each GitHub repository. For example, for homebrew\brew attestations are stored at https://github.com/Homebrew/brew/attestations.

===== Producer


===== Consumer


==== Case study: in-toto Witness

I have not seen this ever and could not find the video mentioned.

Summary attestations, not build attestations.

==== Storage considerations

|                               	| Option 1: Store besides 	| Option 2: Store database 	| Option 3: Store embedded 	| Option 4: Not store 	|
|:-----------------------------:	|-------------------------	|--------------------------	|--------------------------	|---------------------	|
| Discoverability: Attestations 	| ✅                       	| ⚠️                        	|                          	| ❌                   	|
| Discoverability: Data         	| ⚠️                        	| ⚠️                        	|                          	| ❌                   	|


 ✅ = Deals with the consideration, ⚠️ = May deal with the consideration, ❌  = Does not deal with the consideration.

===== Discoverability

Discoverability is defined of how easy is the information to find.
Specifically, a storage consideration is how easy: 1) the attestation is to find given an artifact and 2) the data of the attestation is to find.




Besides artifacts
Databases
Discoverability: in-toto payload
Direct
Direct
Discoverability:
in-toto artifact
Direct
Heuristics



===== Data persistence

How long-lived are the artifacts?
- in-toto payload
- in-toto artifacts
- in-toto references within the artifact

===== Public versus private data

===== Frequency and size (Efficacy? Efficiency?)


=== Consumers

==== Consumption use cases

Immediately before consumption


==== Consumption considerations 

===== Multiple storage mechanisms

A dependency producing in-toto attestations may use multiple storage mechanisms to store data within in-toto. Additionally, different in-toto predicates may store data across different steps within the software supply chain.

An example of such a project is,

Need to find an example project that is good for a particular version.

As such, consumers of in-toto attestations 

===== Duplication

The same attestation may be stored in 

===== Segmented data

Depending on the storage mechanism, the data stored within the attestation may be segmented across different ecosystems. For example, 

===== Normalization

Data within in-toto is stored in different formats.  


===== Notes

- SLSA Verifier -> properties of the certificates, attestations


[[motivation]]
== Motivation

Here, describe the rationale behind the changes you are proposing.

[[reasoning]]
== Reasoning

Here, describe why the decisions you made in the specification make sense.

[[backwards-compatibility]]
== Backwards Compatibility

The current information described in this ITE does not affect or extend in-toto layouts. 
Still, we do outline considerations to take into account when designing in-toto storage for producers. 
Consumers also benefit from standardization, for more standardized consumption and implications when
selecting storage mechanisms.


[[security]]
== Security

Provide a security analysis of these changes including the threat model
considered (and how it differs from the existing threat model for in-toto).
Mention the security impact of adding these changes to in-toto.
